Filter Query Path

The full block-pruning pipeline for a TraceQL filter query. Each stage narrows the candidate blocks before any data is read from storage.

Open Interactive Demo
Step through the 10-stage pruning pipeline with animated block visualization
→

How It Works

When a TraceQL filter query arrives, blockpack runs it through a multi-stage pipeline that progressively narrows the set of blocks before reading any span data. The pipeline is designed for object storage (S3/GCS/Azure) where each I/O request costs 50–100ms of latency, so eliminating blocks before I/O is critical.

Stage 1–3: Preparation (No Pruning)

The query string is parsed into a typed AST, compiled by the VM into a ColumnPredicate closure (for per-span evaluation) and a RangeNode tree (for block-level pruning), then the predicate builder produces a QueryPredicates struct with encoded ranges, bloom keys, and column names.

Stage 4a: File-Level Bloom

Before touching any blocks, blockpack checks a file-level BinaryFuse8 bloom filter over resource.service.name. If the queried service is absent from the entire file, the query returns immediately with zero I/O. This is the FBLM section (magic FBLM).

Stage 4b: Time-Range Reject

Each block carries [MinNano, MaxNano] timestamps. Blocks entirely outside the query’s time window are dropped. This is the cheapest filter — purely metadata comparison, no data access.

Stage 4c: KLL Range Index

At write time, a KLL quantile sketch builds ~1,000 bucket boundaries per numeric column. Each block is assigned to buckets overlapping its [min, max]. At query time, interval matching keeps only blocks with at least one overlapping bucket in the predicate range. For status_code = 500, only blocks whose range includes 500 survive.

Stage 4d: Block Bloom Filters

Each block has a per-column BinaryFuse8 filter over its distinct values. Blocks where the filter definitively rejects a predicate value are dropped. ~0.39% false positive rate.

Stage 4e: CMS Zero-Estimate

For each remaining block, the Count-Min Sketch estimate for each queried value is checked. A CMS estimate of zero means the value was definitively never added — CMS never undercounts. This catches bloom filter false positives.

Stage 5: Block I/O

Each surviving block is read in a single I/O operation via GetBlockWithBytes. The entire block is always read — never a subset of columns. This is the only point where storage I/O occurs.

Stage 6: Column Decode + Span Evaluation

parseBlockColumnsReuse decodes only the columns referenced by the query. The ColumnPredicate closure evaluates the full predicate tree per span row. Matching rows are emitted via SpanMatchCallback.

Pruning in Practice

StageBlocks remainingReduction
Start100—
File bloom8020% of files lack service
Time range4050% outside window
KLL range index1270% have incompatible ranges
Block bloom558% lack the queried value
CMS zero-estimate41 bloom false positive caught
Block I/O496% eliminated before any read

Key Invariant

One I/O per block — always read the entire block. Column filtering is always in-memory.
